definitions/production-possibilities-fro.title: Production Possibilities Frontier
definitions/production-possibilities-fro.description: The production possibilities frontier is a graph representing all possible quantities of products that may be produced when all factors of production are used with perfect efficiency.The X and Y axis each represent the quantity produced of a given commodity. The graph displays the production capacity of one commodity for any given production level of the other.Any point beneath the curve is inefficient. Any point beyond the curve is impossible.The slope of a PPF at any given point is called the Marginal Rate of Transformation. In other words it is the marginal "Opportunity Cost" of a commodity. It measures how much of good Y is given up for one more unit of good X or X for Y. Outward shift = growth in input availability or technological progress Inward shift = contraction in input availability
definitions/production-possibilities-fro.sourcePath: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Definitions/Production Possibilities Frontier.md

graphs/demand-diagonals.html.title: Demand Diagonals
graphs/demand-diagonals.html.description:
This note is shit I don't fully get Demand Diagonals myself to be honest so feel free to make recommendations for this one because its pretty brief It is best to first recall the Production Possibilities Frontier
DD on the Ricardian Model
In the Ricardian model the PPF is a straight line because it assumes that technology is the only factor influencing the opportunity cost. DD is the Demand Diagonal, it represents the ratio an economy wishes to consume a product for a given quantity of the other product. Using the above example the DD is in effect answering the question "If a consumer in this economy is purchasing 5 units of rice, how many motorcycles are they purchasing?"The takeaway (I guess?) Where the Demand Diagonal intercepts the price line (in autarky this is the PPF) is the quantity that the economy will consume of each product under free trade.
Lecture 1 sides 38 & 45

I do my best to use both the jargon we need to know while also including common wording (typically in parentheses).World Trade in the HO Model

graphs/price-level-in-bilateral-trade.ht.title: Price Level in Bilateral trade
graphs/price-level-in-bilateral-trade.ht.description:
Rice trade between Japan and Vietnam The price levels for rice in both economies (PV for Vietnam, PJ for Japan) will converge towards the world price (PW). This will have a different effect in each country. In Japan: The price of rice will fall (decrease) Benefiting Japanese consumers of rice, who will pay a lower price for rice. Hurting Japanese producers of rice, who will be unable to compete with cheaper Vietnamese prices and thus sell less rice. Graphically
Japanese producers will loose area C (Producer Surplus)
Japanese consumers will gain area C and area D (Consumer Surplus) The Japanese economy sees the net gain of area D (this is the gain from trade for Japan) In Vietnam: the price of rice will rise (increase) Benefiting Vietnamese producers of rice, who will sell more rice by undercutting (selling at a lower price) Japanese producers Hurting Vietnamese consumers, who will pay a higher price for rice Graphically
Vietnamese producers will gain area A and area B (Producer Surplus)
Vietnamese consumers will loose area A (Consumer Surplus) The Vietnamese economy sees the net gain of area B (this is the gain from trade for Vietnam) The rule In (bilateral) trade, the economy with the higher autarky price sees a benefit to the consumer, the economy with a lower autarky price a benefit to the producer. The inverse is also true: the economy with a higher autarky price will have its producers hurt, the country with a lower autarky price will have its consumers hurt. The gains to the benefiting party will always outweigh the loss to the hurt party. Thus, the mutual benefits from trade.
Lecture 1 sides 34 & 35
graphs/price-level-in-bilateral-trade.ht.sourcePath: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Graphs/Price Level in Bilateral trade.md

graphs/world-trade-in-the-ho-model.html.title: World Trade in the HO Model
graphs/world-trade-in-the-ho-model.html.description:
It may be helpful to first recall Demand Diagonals, Production Possibilities Frontier and World Trade in the Ricardian Model
World Trade in the HO Model Z = Imports, E = Exports, EVR = Vietnamese Rice Exports, EJM = Japanese Motorcycle Exports, ZVM = Vietnamese Motorcycle Imports, ZJR = Japanese Rice Imports
The Heckscher-Ohlin (HO) model operates effectively the same as the Ricardian model except the PPF is determined in a more realistic (and by extension complex) manner. The HO model has a curved PPF because (amongst other factors) it becomes incredibly difficult to use every resource for rice production instead of motorcycle production (Ex. how do you use a welding torch for rice cultivation instead of making a motorcycle?).
For this reason, point B is no longer a country putting ALL of it's resources towards the production of the product it has comparative advantage in. Instead it finds the most efficient level of production based on the world price of the product. B is plotted where the world price line (the dashed one) intersects the PPF.But why does Vietnam import a minority of its motorcycles when Japan is still importing most of it's rice? If you look at the curve of the PPFs you might notice that Japan's is sharper than Vietnam's. This represents that Japan has a higher opportunity cost for producing rice instead of motorcycles. They produce vastly more motorcycles than rice for the same amount of resources. Vietnam on the other hand has a more balanced opportunity cost. Even when producing at point B (most efficient based on the world price), they still have enough resources to fulfill most of their motorcycle demand. The remainder (ZVM) is then imported. When Japan produces at it's point B it has practically no resources left for rice rice production, meaning it then has to import the majority (ZJR)
Lecture 1 sides 53-64
graphs/world-trade-in-the-ho-model.html.sourcePath: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Graphs/World Trade in the HO Model.md

graphs/world-trade-in-the-ricardian-mode.title: World Trade in the Ricardian Model
graphs/world-trade-in-the-ricardian-mode.description:
It may be helpful to first recall Demand Diagonals and Production Possibilities Frontier
World Trade in the Riccardian Model
DD: the Demand Diagonal
Unlabeled solid: the Production Possibilities Frontier (and by extension price line) in autarky Dashed line: the price line with world trade Point A represents consumption in autarky
This point is on the Demand Diagonal because that is where the economy is in line with consumer demand Point B represents complete specialization in a single product In these cases that is motorcycles for Japan and rice for Vietnam because that is what they each respectively have a comparative advantage in. Point C represents consumption when each economy trades its excess products to the other
This enables them to both consume beyond their domestic PPF.
This point is on the Demand Diagonal because that is where the economy is in line with consumer demand
Measuring Imports and Exports Z = Imports, E = Exports, CVR = Vietnamese Rice Consumption, CJM = Japanese Motorcycle Consumption, ZVM = Vietnamese Motorcycle Imports, ZJR = Japanese Rice Imports (his graph labels kinda suck ass here so bear with me)In the above graph we assume that each country is producing at point B (see previous section). Vietnam will consume rice until point C (CVR), Japan will consume motorcycles until point C (CJM). All additional product, not consumed domestically, is then exported (represented by EJM and EVR respectively). Since each country is putting all if its resources into its most efficiently produced product (producing at B), they import all of their consumption of the other product. For Vietnam this means their consumption of motorcycles is entirely imported (ZVM). For Japan this means their consumption of rice is entirely imported (ZJR).
Lecture 1 sides 43-52
graphs/world-trade-in-the-ricardian-mode.sourcePath: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Graphs/World Trade in the Ricardian Model.md

graphs/quota-graph.html.title: Quota Graph
graphs/quota-graph.html.description:
It may be beneficial to first recall the Tariff Graph
Effects of a quota on Japan's imports of rice *Key: PW = World Price, Pquota = Price with quota, ZJ = Japanese rice imports, ZJquota = Japanese imports with quota, QS = Quantity supplied without quota QD = Quantity demanded without quota, QSquota = Quantity supplied with quota, QDquota = Quantity demanded with quota (all in terms of rice)Rather than directly imposing a price increase like a tariff does by adding a tax on imports, a quota limits the quantity which may be imported. But ultimately the effect is nearly the same in terms of the graph. The limited supply of cheaper foreign goods drives prices up. Quotas are regulated by licensing to either foreign suppliers or domestic importers
The remainder of this note will look very similar to Tariff Graph. Key differences will be emphasized with ALL CAPS
When the price is at PW, shapes A, B, C, and D are all part of Consumer Surplus.When the price is at Pquota:
A: Producer Surplus domestic producers regain the ability to sell to individuals who are willing to purchase rice at this higher price
B: Deadweight loss C: DEPENDS ON IMPLEMENTATION OF LICENSING If domestic importers get the licensing for foreign goods they gain C If foreign suppliers get the licensing they gain C
D: Deadweight loss He emphasized this a lot in lectures
When a quota is imposed the price shifts from PW to Pquota. In the process, areas B and D are lost and not gained by anyone. Hence why they are called deadweight loss Without the tariff this is part of Consumer Surplus.
Suggestion I would suggest consulting the summary of welfare effects of both tariffs and quotas on page 28 of lecture 2
Lecture 2 slides 24-28

graphs/tariff-graph.html.title: Tariff Graph
graphs/tariff-graph.html.description:
Effects of a tariff on Japan's imports of rice Key: PW = World Price, T = Tariff, ZJ = Japanese rice imports, ZJtariff = Japanese imports with tariff, QS = Quantity supplied without tariff QD = Quantity demanded without tariff, QStariff = Quantity supplied with tariff, QDtariff = Quantity demanded with tariff (all in terms of rice)
When the price is at PW, shapes A, B, C, and D are all part of Consumer Surplus. When the price is at PW + T:
A: Producer Surplus domestic producers regain the ability to sell to individuals who are willing to purchase rice at this higher price
B: Deadweight loss C: Revenue for the government by collecting the tariff
D: Deadweight loss He emphasized this a lot in lectures
When a tariff is imposed the price shifts from PW to PW + T. In the process, areas B and D are lost and not gained by anyone. Hence why they are called deadweight loss. Without the tariff this is part of Consumer Surplus. The Net Welfare Effect (N), can be represented by the following equation.
Suggestion I would suggest consulting the summary of welfare effects of both tariffs and quotas on page 28 of lecture 2
Lecture 2 slides 20-23

graphs/trade-creating-pta.html.title: Trade Creating PTA
graphs/trade-creating-pta.html.description:
This note will not reexplain the Tariff Graph and will assume you are reasonably well versed in it
Trade Creating Preferential Trade Agreement effect on Brazil I have added colors here to make the shapes more readable Key: ZB = Brazilian imports, ZBPTA = Brazilian imports with PTA, PA = Price in Argentina, PS = Prince in El Salvador, T = Tariff (the domestic price in Brazil under autarky is found at the intersection of SB and DB)*Assume Brazil currently has tariffs on both Argentinian and El Salvadorian imports. In this case Brazil would like to trade with Argentina because, even with tariffs, they have the lowest price. Reducing or removing tariffs on both countries equally would lead Brazil to still trade with Argentina, though the volume (amount) traded will increase due to the lower price. This is trade creation by removing tariffs with a preferential trade agreement.
The Consumer Surplus gained is shapes ABCD. It is NOT effected by El Salvadors price level with or without tariffs. This is because the lower bound of prices for this product is always determined by the lowest price, which El Salvador does not have in either case.**Gains and loss for the Brazilian economy by signing this PTA A
Gain: Consumer Surplus
Loss: Producer Surplus B
Gain: Consumer Surplus Loss: none C
Gain: Consumer Surplus Loss: Government tariff revenue D
Gain: Consumer Surplus Loss: none Net welfare is a gain of B + D
Lecture 2 slides 46-49
graphs/trade-creating-pta.html.sourcePath: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Graphs/Trade Creating PTA.md

graphs/trade-diverting-pta.html.title: Trade-Diverting PTA
graphs/trade-diverting-pta.html.description:
It may be beneficial to first recall Trade Creating PTAs
Trade-Diverting Preferential Trade Agreement effect on Brazil Key: *ZB = Brazilian imports, ZBPTA = Brazilian imports with PTA, PA = Price in Argentina, PS = Prince in El Salvador, T = Tariff (the domestic price in Brazil under autarky is found at the intersection of SB and DB)Assume Brazil currently has tariffs on both Argentinian and El Salvadorian imports. El Salvador in this case has the lowest cost for this product. Trade diversion occurs when Brazil makes a PTA with Argentina, who has a higher cost. Before the PTA (i.e. with equal tariffs) it is expected that Brazil was trading with El Salvador because PS + T was the lowest price available. But with a PTA between Argentina and Brazil, Brazil will begin to trade with Argentina instead. This is the trade being diverted from El Salvador to Argentina because they are subject to different tariffs.
In this graph Brazil is gaining A, B, C, and D as Consumer Surplus. They are loosing A as Producer Surplus. They also loose both C and E which was the tariffs from El Salvador that they are no longer collecting because trade with El Salvador (theoretically) ends for this product. The net gain is B + D, but you will notice that those are quite small so E (the larger area) outweighs it. This results in a net loss from this trade diversion that does not happen with trade creation.
Lecture 2 slides 50 & 51

definitions/consumer-surplus.html.title: Consumer Surplus
definitions/consumer-surplus.html.description: Consumer Surplus is the material gain for consumers (those purchasing a good or service), obtained when they can purchase a product for a price lower than the absolute most they would willing purchase it for.

definitions/producer-surplus.html.title: Producer Surplus
definitions/producer-surplus.html.description: Producer Surplus is the difference between the minimum price that producers (those who manufacture or otherwise sell a good or service) would be willing to sell at, and the market price that is higher than that minimum.

definitions/the-impossible-trinity.html.title: The Impossible Trinity
definitions/the-impossible-trinity.html.description:
The Impossible Trinity The impossible trinity is a theory regarding exchange rates and monetary policy. It is called the impossible trinity because, while countries want all 3, the laws of economics make it so that only 2 out of 3 are possible at any one time.Each side of the triangle represents what economies want. Everybody (in theory) wants: Monetary independence Gives them more control in managing their domestic economy Exchange rate stability Creates certainty for business conducting cross-border deals. They can be certain they will be paid at the same value because the currency will not increase/decrease in value unexpectedly changing their return on investment. Capital mobility to enable foreign savings and investment Capital Mobility & Monetary Independence Cannot have exchange rate stability Why? adjusting interest rates (monetary policy) and allowing capital to flow across your border freely, will result in capital rushing into and out of your economy as it hunts for the most favorable interest rate, in doing so the exchange rate Monetary Independence & Exchange Rate Stability Cannot have capital mobility Why? keeping your currency at a fixed price (exchange rate stability) while also setting your own interest rates (monetary independence) means that you cannot allow people to trade your currency freely on the open market as that would cause Exchange Rate Stability & Capital Mobility Cannot have monetary independence Why? If you allow money to flow freely in and out of your economy and you refuse to let the exchange rate change your interest rate must be matched with a country whose currency yours is pegged to.
Lecture 5 slides 58-60
definitions/the-impossible-trinity.html.sourcePath: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Definitions/The Impossible Trinity.md

definitions/black-wednesday-(1992).html.title: Black Wednesday (1992)
definitions/black-wednesday-(1992).html.description: Black Wednesday was a Speculative Attack on the Great British pound that occurred on September 16th, 1992. Fears over the Brit's ability to to deal with the ongoing recession while under ERM restrictions led to mass sell offs of the pound sterling that were further enabled by the liberalized capital controls of EMU stage 1.

definitions/generations-of-currency-cris.title: Generations of Currency Crisis
definitions/generations-of-currency-cris.description:
Economics scholarship classifies currency crises into 3 generationsA government with a currency peg is spending more than it earns (budget deficit) and prints money to cover this deficit. Attempting to account for the ensuing inflation begins to drain the central bank's foreign reserves until a collapse seems inevitable and investors engage in a Speculative Attack.Example Mexico consistently had large budget deficits in the 70s and 80s. To fund this deficit, they expanded the domestic money supply. Because they were trying to keep their exchange rate fixed to the U.S. Dollar, they bought up a lot of their currency on forex markets to try to maintain the exchange rate. As investors saw foreign reserves running out, they launched a speculative attack which served as the tipping point that forced the government to devalue.
Investors believe that the government will not maintain a currency peg as it becomes increasingly politically controversial to do so. If they predict this as coming they will launch a Speculative Attack and this will serve as a self fulfilling prophecy of sorts as this makes it even more difficult to maintain a currency peg.
Example The UK was a part of the European Exchange Rate Mechanism (ERM) which pegged the Pound to the German Mark. As Germany raised inflation rates, the UK had to follow suit even though they were in a deep recession and faced rising unemployment. Investors (Jeff loves to name George Soros) bet that the British government would not hold on to the peg as their population suffered. With this expectation they sold billions of pounds in what came to be called Black Wednesday (1992). This worsened the situation and led to the UK abandoning the ERM peg the same day. Private banks borrow foreign currencies (USD for example) but earn revenue in their domestic currency. If the value of their domestic currency goes down even just a little compared to the USD, their debt quickly becomes almost impossible to pay.Example Thai banks in the 90s borrowed massive amounts of USD because American interest rates were low, they felt secure in the Thai currency peg to the dollar. But when the peg was abandoned in response to a real estate bubble burst, banks and other firms who had borrowed USD suddenly needed way more of the Thai currency than expected to pay back the same amount of USD.
definitions/generations-of-currency-cris.sourcePath: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Definitions/Generations of Currency Crisis.md

definitions/speculative-attack.html.title: Speculative Attack
definitions/speculative-attack.html.description: A speculative attack is a sudden and large selling of an asset that is deemed unlikely to hold its value going forward. The term is most closely associated with foreign exchange markets and the selling of a nation's currency.

definitions/absolute-advantage.html.title: Absolute Advantage
definitions/absolute-advantage.html.description:
One country can produce a product more efficiently than another (i.e. more cheaply). Therefore, that country will export to another country (assuming it is allowed to).Rice in Vietnam and Japan
Vietnam may export rice at a price cheaper than Japan can produce domestically and hence export rice to Japan. Some may go hungry in Vietnam. Some economists justify this by saying the inflow of capital, rising wages, investment in industry and infrastructure, etc., will initiate a reinforcing cycle of development. In this way Vietnam entered the global economy. When the supply curve for country A is to the right of Country B's supply curve it means that at every price Country A supplies more of that commodity at every price.

definitions/balance-of-payments.html.title: Balance of Payments
definitions/balance-of-payments.html.description:
The balance of payments (BOP) is effectively an economies accounting book. Put simply, it is the record of every economic transaction that crosses a countries borders.What BOP is NOT
The balance of payments is NOT a measure of how much money a government has. While the balance of payments may have effects on a countries treasury, it is not the same. To make the difference clear:
The state (i.e. the government, the administration, the ministry of finance/department of the treasury) has what is effectively the state's own bank account.
The economy (i.e. the sum of every actor within a state's borders like individuals, firms, and the state itself). The BOP is the flow of capital in and out of the economy.
As the word "balance" suggests, the BOP can be represented by an equation that should ideally result in 0:The current account tracks the flow of goods, services, and income in the economy. It can be divided into 4 components:
Balance of Trade: the amount of physical goods imported subtracted from the amount of physical goods exported (exports-imports of goods)
Services: trade like tourism, banking, insurance, and transport.
Primary income: Earning on investments. Importantly it does not matter where these investments are held. An American owning stock in a German company still receives their dividend in the US so it is recorded here.
Secondary income: Single transfer events like receiving money from an individual overseas (often in the form remittances: economic migrants sending money home which makes up a significant portions of some economies current accounts)
The Rule
If an economy has a positive Current Account they are a net lender, meaning they save more money than they spend (and by extension loan it out). If their current account is negative they are a net borrower, they spend more than they have and must borrow.
The financial account tracks the "paper economy", meaning the transfer of certain financial assets. It is concerned with what you own. It is similar to the way the value of your home or an expensive piece of jewelry is a part of your net worth despite not being cash you can spend. The assets it tracks may take the form of:
Foreign Direct Investment (FDI): Owning a significant (>10%) share of a foreign company, or physical capital like a factory in a foreign country.
Portfolio Investment: Buying stocks (<10% of the company), bonds, crypto currency. Note that this is not the same as primary income in the current account because there we are concerned with what is returned (the cash made) from the asset. Here it is the ownership of that asset, the value of what is owned.
Other Investments: Bank loans, currency deposits, etc. Loans to other countries are assets, an actor in your economy owns that debt. Loans to an actor in your economy from another economy is a liability. You owe them the money borrowed. Currency deposits act the same way. If a British company has a deposit in a German Bank, that German bank has a liability to that company to give it that money upon request and vice versa. Reserve Assets: The Central Bank's holdings of assets like gold and foreign currencies (also in a few cases crypto but don't worry about that). These reserves are used to defend a currency peg or otherwise steer your currencies value on foreign exchange (forex) markets.
The capital account is super minor and usually very small in modern accounting. It covers the transfer of patents, copyrights, or debt forgiveness.The Current Account and the Financial Account are mirrors of each other. Meaning that if one is negative (a deficit) the other must be positive (a surplus).
Current Account Deficit: This means you are buying more goods and services from abroad than you are selling to other countries. Obviously this raises the question "How do you pay for that?". The answer is that you must sell assets such as a piece of your company (stocks/FDI), government debt (bonds), or land to foreign holders. This results in a Financial Account Surplus, an inflow of money to your economy. This money is then used to import the goods and services.
Current Account Surplus: This means you are selling more goods and services than you buy meaning that you end up with extra liquid capital (cash). This is then used to buy foreign stocks, factories, or government debt, meaning a Financial Account Deficit.

headers.heading: The Relationship Between Current and Financial Accounts

definitions/balance-of-payments.html.sourcePath: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Definitions/Balance of Payments.md

definitions/comparative-advantage.html.title: Comparative Advantage
definitions/comparative-advantage.html.description:
Absolute Advantage works in a one product model. Comparative Advantage assumes a two product model. This is the logical next step because countries would not want to accept imports if it could not reciprocate and export things to other countries as well. This is because countries desire a healthy Balance of Payments (i.e. balanced inflows and outflows of capital).
As graphed on the Production Possibilities Frontier there are tradeoffs that countries make in order to produce one unit of a commodity in terms of another commodity. For example to make one motorcycle you forgo making 3 units of rice, but another country only forgoes 2 units of rice per motorcycle. This trade off of X amount of one item produced for every Y amount of another is the Opportunity Cost. If you have the lower opportunity cost (in this case 2 rice per motorcycle) you have the comparative advantage.

definitions/the-international-finance-eq.title: The International Finance Equations
definitions/the-international-finance-eq.description:
International Finance is at it's heart about the exchange of assets across borders. These equations are used to keep track of this flow, aka the practice of accounting.The Key SH = Household savings SG = Government savings SF = Foreign savings I = Investment in domestic firms E = Exports Z = Imports In effect this is saying that the investment into your economies firms/companies (I), is equal to the amount of domestic savings (SH + SG, because the government and households are both domestic actors), in addition to foreign savings SF.This equation can then of course be transformed to determine another variable. If you wanted to determine how much foreign wealth was saved in your country you could solve the following. This equation is subtracting domestic savings (SH + SG) from domestic investment (I), leaving us with the foreign savings (SF).Read more about current accounts in the note on Balance of Payments This equation is adding the economies exports (E) to the foreign savings (SF) to determine how much is imported (Z). This is because when you import more than you export, those extra exports are paid for by foreign savings/investments. Naturally you can also find uses for transforming this one such as determining foreign savings using your imports and exports like so. With Z - E representing the trade deficit, it could also represent a surplus if exports (E) is greater than imports (Z).
Lecture 4 slides 9-14
definitions/the-international-finance-eq.sourcePath: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Definitions/The International Finance Equations.md

graphs/exchange-rate-graphs.html.title: Exchange Rate Graphs
graphs/exchange-rate-graphs.html.description:
This will be a somewhat long one. I am going to do my best to hold-hand as much as possible and explain the graphs step by step, slowly increasing the complexity because while the final product looks intimidating it is really basic when you strip away the bullshit. So bear with me.Supply and Demand Graph At the end of the day exchange rates are determined by what is (if you simplify it enough) supply and demand. So keep in mind for me that price is the Y axis, quantity is the X axis, supply is the inclined line and demand is the declined line.The nominal exchange rate (e) is the value of your currency divided by another nations currency. To put names to them (and match the lecture example) we will use Mexican pesos and US dollars. If e were to increase this would represent a depreciation of the peso (a fall in it's value). If e were to decrease this would represent an appreciation (a rise in it's value).Why? because when you divide 1 by e the relation to appreciation and depreciation flips which makes it more intuitive (natural feeling and logical) on a graph. With 1/e higher values represents an appreciation and a lower value a depreciation. Just remember that for e on it's own this is the opposite and we use 1/e to make it nicer on a graph.Example If 1 USD is .80 CAD (Canadian dollars) then we can plug in .8 for e (because .80/1 = .8) and then use that in our 1/e equation to determine the following In practice this means that we can now draw the below Y axis
1/e charted on a Y axis
Exchange Rate Graph So now we get to our exchange rate graph. Lets say for the peso. Notice that our Y axis is charting 1/e. While it does not say Price (P), that is still technically what it means. The exchange rate is the price to buy a currency using another. At the end of the day both price and exchange rates are a way to represent value. Value is what is charted on the Y in supply and demand as well as exchange rate graphs.The points on the Y axis are as follows: 1/e0 = equilibrium price of the peso (the value it 'desires' according to economic theory) 1/e1 = this represents an overvalued peso. Its value is more than equilibrium meaning there is excess supply (which is illustrated on the graph) that will make it easier to obtain pesos, hence driving its value down (depreciation). 1/e2 = this represents an undervalued peso. Its value is less than equilibrium meaning there is excess demand which will make pesos more scarce (harder to get), hence driving its value up (appreciation). Q is still at the end of the day Quantity but there is a more complex way to represent this
This model represents how trade deficits can affect the movement of exchange rates. For this we use one of The International Finance Equations (review that note for the background on this): What this equation means is that, in this case Mexico's, trade deficit will be paid for by foreign savings (SF). Now let us look at this on the graph.
Trade-Based Model of Exchange Rates Notice that Q has turned into Z-E (aka SF). This shows that as the trade deficit (importing more than you export) increases, so does the supply of pesos. But when Mexicans buy imported products, from the USA for example, they are demanding fewer pesos because those products are purchased with dollars (at the time of import). So as the trade deficit increases, the amount of pesos available also increases.Lets add the demand curve back in. You will see it is perfectly vertical because it is (in theory) perfectly inelastic.
Trade Based Exchange Rate Graph with a shift in Demand This is representing a growth in foreign savings (FS) which causes the currency to appreciate (increase in value). The new equilibrium is the intersection between the dashed line (the new demand for pesos) and the supply of pesos. The vertical line on the left is the demand for pesos before an inflow of foreign savings (SF), the dashed line is the demand for pesos after an inflow of foreign savings. An inflow of foreigns savings increases demand because savings in Mexican accounts (or other investments) are made using pesos. Almost there stick with me. We now assume that currency is seen as an asset. In the trade based model we only consider it a means by which to buy other goods and services. Now we consider it as an asset because it is something that can appreciate or depreciate and hence something which can be invested in. All that is to say that investors will purchase the peso if they either: Expect the value of the peso to go up (relative to the peso) Expect their own currency to go down (relative to the peso) They expect a more favorable exchange rate. When we consider currency to be an asset, demand once again becomes elastic, meaning the line can once again be drawn on a decline.
Asset Based Exchange Rate Graph If the price of the currency is currently at 1/e2, then investors want to invest in the currency because the excess demand will cause appreciation (represented by the upward arrow point to 1/e0).If the price of the currency is currently at 1/e1, anyone who is currently invested in the currency will want to sell off because the excess supply will cause the currency to depreciate (represented by the downward arrow pointing to 1/e0)If you invest in the peso when the exchange rate is 1 peso per 1 dollar, and then the value appreciates to be 1.1 peso per 1 dollar, you have gained 10%, in this case 10 cents. But if you invested $1,000,000 that 10% gain becomes a net gain of $100,000.

info_showCustomIcons.description: Show custom icons for files and folders

info_showDefaultFolderIcons.description: Show a default icon of a folder for every folder in the tree

info_showDefaultFileIcons.description: Show a default icon of a file for every file in the tree

info_defaultFolderIcon.description: The icon to use for folders. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_defaultFileIcon.description: The icon to use for files. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_defaultMediaIcon.description: The icon to use for media files. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_exposeStartingPath.description: Whether or not to show the current file in the file tree when the page is first loaded

info_displayTitle.description: Descriptive title to show above the feature

info_showOrphanNodes.description: Show nodes that are not connected to any other nodes.

info_showAttachments.description: Show attachments like images and PDFs as nodes in the graph.

info_allowGlobalGraph.description: Allow the user to view the global graph of all nodes.

info_allowExpand.description: Allow the user to pop-out the graph view to take up the whole screen

info_attractionForce.description: How much should linked nodes attract each other? This will make the graph appear more clustered.

info_linkLength.description: How long should the links between nodes be? The shorter the links the more connected nodes will cluster together.

info_repulsionForce.description: How much should nodes repel each other? This will make disconnected parts more spread out.

info_centralForce.description: How much should nodes be attracted to the center? This will make the graph appear more dense and circular.

info_edgePruning.description: Edges with a length above this threshold will not be rendered, however they will still contribute to the simulation. This can help large tangled graphs look more organised. Hovering over a node will still display these links.

info_minNodeRadius.description: How small should the smallest nodes be? The smaller a node is the less it will attract other nodes.

info_maxNodeRadius.description: How large should the largest nodes be? Nodes are sized by how many links they have. The larger a node is the more it will attract other nodes. This can be used to create a good grouping around the most important nodes.

info_allowResizing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be resized

info_allowCollapsing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be collapsed

info_rightDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the right sidebar

info_leftDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the left sidebar

info_selector.description: CSS selector for an element. The feature will be placed relative to this element.

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_sourcePath.description: The local path to the source .html file which will be included.

info_allowFoldingLists.description: Whether or not to allow lists to be folded

info_allowFoldingHeadings.description: Whether or not to allow headings to be folded

info_siteUrl.description: The url that this site will be hosted at

info_authorName.description: The name of the author of the site
